did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning models, specifically DenseNet-121 with an Attention Mechanism and U-Net for segmentation, along with the Improved Cat Swarm Optimization Algorithm (ICSOA) for hyperparameter tuning, to perform skin cancer detection and classification on dermatological datasets (ISIC 2017-2020, PH2, HAM10000).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel 'Attention Mechanism with DenseNet-121' classifier optimized by the 'Improved Cat Swarm Optimization Algorithm (ICSOA)'. It details specific methodology including U-Net segmentation and Gray World Color Constancy, reports new empirical results with accuracy metrics (e.g., 99.87% on ISIC 2017) on public datasets (ISIC 2017-2020, PH2, HAM10000), and includes statistical analysis and ablation studies, distinguishing it from a review paper.

title: An Efficient Skin Cancer Detection and Classification Using DenseNet-121 with Attention Mechanism
journal: International Journal of Intelligent Engineering and Systems
brief_summary: This study proposes an efficient skin cancer detection and classification method using a DenseNet-121 classifier integrated with an Attention Mechanism (AM) and optimized by the Improved Cat Swarm Optimization Algorithm (ICSOA). The methodology includes Gray World Color Constancy for color normalization and U-Net for lesion segmentation to address challenges like poor contrast and redundant features. The model was evaluated on public dermatological datasets, including ISIC 2017-2020, PH2, and HAM10000. Comparative analysis demonstrated that the proposed ICSOA-DenseNet121 model outperformed existing approaches such as InsiNet, N-DNN, and Skin-Net. Key results included classification accuracies of 99.87%, 99.83%, 99.72%, and 99.55% on the ISIC 2017-2020 datasets, respectively, and 99.67% on the PH2 dataset. The study concludes that the compact architecture effectively mitigates overfitting while achieving superior diagnostic accuracy.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant, and specific lesion classes such as Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a DenseNet-121 classifier with an Attention Mechanism to predict discrete diagnostic labels for skin lesions. The model is evaluated on datasets like ISIC 2017-2020, PH2, and HAM10000, reporting classification accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and F1-score. The task involves distinguishing between benign and malignant lesions and identifying specific cancer types.

all_task_types.target: Lesion masks (binary masks highlighting the region of interest within the skin image)
all_task_types.details: The methodology employs a U-Net architecture for lesion segmentation to identify individual skin lesions and remove redundant features. Performance metrics for this task include Jaccard index, Dice coefficient, and segmentation accuracy (e.g., 99.52% for U-Net).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image collection from ISIC comprising images and associated ground truths for skin cancer analysis.

use.testing: Used for model evaluation/testing (20% test split provided by dataset).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from ISIC comprising dermoscopic training images from over 2,000 individuals with various benign and malignant skin diseases.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset used as an additional testing set for models trained on ISIC datasets, containing dermoscopy images of melanoma and naevus.

use.testing: Used as an additional testing set.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: The Human Against Machine through 10000 training images dataset, a widely used collection of dermoscopy images for skin lesion classification.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To confirm the statistical superiority of the proposed DenseNet-121 model over existing approaches (ViT, LeNet, ZFNet, GoogLeNet) by comparing performance metrics.
To validate the significant performance improvements of the proposed model compared to state-of-the-art methods, providing statistical confidence.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in performance between the proposed DenseNet-121 model and existing approaches (ViT, LeNet, ZFNet, GoogLeNet).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: The proposed DenseNet-121 model has significantly better performance than existing approaches.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The statistical analysis confirmed the superiority of DenseNet-121, with the lowest p-values and highest t-values, highlighting significant performance improvements over other models and validating that DenseNet-121 outperforms ViT, LeNet, ZFNet, and GoogLeNet with strong statistical confidence.

architecture_details.attention_mechanism: Channel Attention Mechanism (AM) that modifies weights of feature maps in the channel domain, combining transition layers and dense block without introducing extra parameters. Includes 'squeeze' and 'excitation' phases where input features are squeezed into a 1-D vector.
architecture_details.segmentation_module: U-Net (used for lesion segmentation prior to classification)
architecture_details.optimization_algorithm: Improved Cat Swarm Optimization Algorithm (ICSOA) used for hyperparameter tuning
architecture_details.preprocessing: Gray World Color Constancy for color normalization

training_details.hyperparameters: Tuned via ICSOA (Improved Cat Swarm Optimization Algorithm)

classification_or_output_head.details: Selected significant features from the attention mechanism are fed into the classification layer. The model performs binary classification (benign vs. malignant) and multi-class classification (specific lesion types).

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: N-DNN (Neutrosophic Features with a Deep Neural Network)
preprocessing_techniques: Gray World Color Constancy method for color normalization across input images
Subtraction of dataset's mean intensity RGB value from each channel to further normalize data and reduce noise impact
U-Net segmentation to isolate individual skin lesions and remove redundant features/artifacts (e.g., hair, skin surface reflections)
conclusions: The proposed ICSOA-DenseNet121 model, which integrates an Attention Mechanism with DenseNet-121 and uses the Improved Cat Swarm Optimization Algorithm (ICSOA) for hyperparameter tuning, achieves superior skin cancer classification accuracy compared to existing approaches such as InsiNet, N-DNN, and Skin-Net.
The model achieved high classification accuracies of 99.87%, 99.83%, 99.72%, and 99.55% on the ISIC 2017-2020 datasets, and 99.67% on the PH2 dataset.
The compact and efficient architecture of the proposed model effectively mitigates the overfitting issue commonly associated with channel attention networks.

DenseNet-121 outperforms state-of-the-art methods (including ViT, LeNet, ZFNet, and GoogLeNet) due to its efficient feature reuse, reduced number of parameters, and effective gradient flow, which enhance learning especially with constrained data.
Statistical analysis (p-test and t-test) confirms the significant performance superiority of the proposed DenseNet-121 model over other models with strong statistical confidence.
The proposed DenseNet-121 model demonstrates significantly lower training time, faster inference speed, and reduced memory usage compared to other deep learning approaches across all tested datasets.
The U-Net segmentation module effectively isolates individual skin lesions and removes redundant features, achieving a segmentation accuracy of 99.52%, which outperforms AlexNet, VGG-Net, and ResNet.
The ablation study confirms that the configuration using DenseNet-121 with ICSOA outperforms configurations without ICSOA or with random hyperparameter tuning.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on retrospective public datasets (ISIC 2017-2020, PH2, HAM10000) with no external clinical validation or prospective testing, limiting generalizability to real-world clinical settings.
The PH2 dataset used for external validation is small (200 images) and heavily imbalanced (160 Naevus vs. 40 Melanoma), which may inflate performance metrics for the benign class.
The model was implemented in MATLAB R2020a on specific hardware (16GB RAM, Intel i7), which may not reflect performance on standard GPU-accelerated clinical deployment environments.

Preprocessing steps, including Gray World Color Constancy and U-Net segmentation, introduce potential data leakage risks if segmentation masks were derived from the entire dataset statistics rather than strictly per-fold during cross-validation.

The paper does not report per-class metrics for the multi-class HAM10000 dataset, preventing assessment of performance on rare or clinically critical subtypes like Melanoma versus specific benign lesions.

The comparison with state-of-the-art methods (ViT, LeNet, etc.) lacks detailed statistical confidence intervals for the accuracy differences, relying only on p-values and t-values without specifying the alpha level or effect sizes.

The proposed method's reliance on U-Net for segmentation prior to classification assumes perfect segmentation; errors in the segmentation phase could propagate to the classification task, though this error rate is not quantified.
